channel.title: Blockchain Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Blockchain Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Autonomous World
item.description:
An autonomous world is a virtual environment whose state, rules, and asset ownership are stored entirely on a public blockchain, persisting and evolving independently of any single developer, company, or server.

Ludens (co-founder of Lattice, the team behind the MUD framework) articulated the concept in August 2022, identifying three essential conditions: autonomy (the world runs without a central operator), permissionless creation (anyone can extend the world’s mechanics without approval), and physical law adherence (the world has coherent, stable, transparent rules).

item.title: Composability
item.description:
Composability is the property of a system where its components can be freely combined, recombined, and built upon by anyone, without coordination with or permission from the original creators. In blockchain systems, composability means any smart contract can call any other smart contract’s public functions, read any piece of on-chain state, and build new functionality on top of existing protocols.

The term draws an analogy to Unix pipes, LEGO bricks, or API composition — but blockchain composability is stronger than any of these because it operates on shared, permissionless state. A Unix program can pipe data to another, but both must run on the same machine under the same user’s control. A blockchain contract can interact with any other contract deployed by anyone, at any time, without prior agreement.

item.title: Consensus Mechanism
item.description:
A consensus mechanism is the protocol by which nodes in a distributed network reach agreement on a single canonical version of the ledger’s on-chain state, without requiring a trusted central coordinator.

The problem a consensus mechanism solves is fundamental: in a network of thousands of independent computers, each receiving transactions in different orders at different times, how does the network agree on which transactions are valid and in what sequence? A centralized database solves this trivially — one server is authoritative. A decentralized network needs a protocol that tolerates dishonest participants, network delays, and conflicting claims.

item.title: Content Identifier (CID)
item.description:
A Content Identifier (CID) is a label used in IPFS to point to material based on the content itself, not where the content is stored. Identifying material by the value of its hash is called content addressing.

A CID encodes:

  • The hash function used (typically SHA256)
  • The hash value itself
  • The data structure type (DAG node, raw file, etc.)

When you add a file to IPFS, each chunk is given a unique CID. The root node’s CID becomes the file’s identifier — what you share with others to retrieve it.

item.title: Decentralized Autonomous Organization (DAO)
item.description:
A decentralized autonomous organization (DAO) is an entity whose rules of operation are encoded in smart contracts on a blockchain, with decisions made through token-weighted voting by its members rather than by a centralized board or executive.

Vitalik Buterin outlined the concept in 2014, distinguishing it from simpler constructs: a decentralized application (DA) has a protocol but no governance; a decentralized organization (DO) has governance by humans using a blockchain for coordination; a DAO replaces the human governance layer with autonomous code. In a DAO, the rules execute automatically — funds are released, proposals are enacted, and members are admitted or removed according to the contract logic, not managerial discretion.

item.title: Decentralized Governance
item.description:
Decentralized governance is the set of processes by which stakeholders in a blockchain protocol or DAO make collective decisions — parameter changes, treasury allocations, protocol upgrades — without a central authority holding veto power.

In traditional organizations, governance flows through boards, executives, and shareholders with legally defined roles. In decentralized systems, governance is encoded in smart contracts: proposals are submitted on-chain, votes are cast by token holders, and approved proposals execute automatically.

Common mechanisms:

item.title: Fog of War
item.description:
Fog of war is a game mechanic where the information available to a player is limited to what they have discovered or can see from their current vantage point. It creates uncertainty and the need for exploration.

In traditional games, the server knows all player positions and chooses what to reveal to each client. A cheater can modify the client to request all data.

In cryptographic fog of war (used in Dark Forest), the blockchain itself cannot see hidden information. Players commit to positions by hashing them, and only through brute-force computation can they discover other players’ positions.

item.title: Gas Fee
item.description:
A gas fee is the cost a user pays to execute a transaction on a blockchain. On Ethereum, every operation — transferring tokens, calling a smart contract function, storing data — consumes a measurable quantity of gas, and users pay for that gas in ETH.

Gas serves two purposes: it compensates validators for the computational resources they expend processing transactions, and it prevents abuse by making denial-of-service attacks economically prohibitive. Without gas, an attacker could submit infinite loops or spam transactions at no cost, overwhelming the network.

item.title: Merkle Root
item.description:
A Merkle root is the final hash computed at the top of a Merkle tree. It is the result of recursively hashing pairs of adjacent data items until a single hash remains.

Properties:

  1. Fingerprint: The Merkle root is a compact representation of the entire tree. If even one leaf changes, the root changes completely.

  2. Verification: You can prove that a specific leaf is in the tree by providing a Merkle proof — a logarithmic number of hashes along the path from that leaf to the root. The verifier recomputes hashes upward and checks they match the root.

item.title: Non-Fungible Token (NFT)
item.description:
A non-fungible token (NFT) is a token where each unit carries a unique identifier, making it distinguishable from every other token in the same contract. Unlike fungible tokens (where one unit equals any other), each NFT represents a specific asset — a piece of art, a game character, a plot of virtual land, a membership credential.

The ERC-721 standard, authored by William Entriken, Dieter Shirley, Jacob Evans, and Nastassia Sachs in January 2018, formalized this pattern on Ethereum. The contract maps each token ID to exactly one owner address and provides functions for transferring individual tokens. The standard also includes a metadata extension: each token ID can point to a URI describing the asset’s properties (name, image, attributes).

item.title: On-Chain State
item.description:
On-chain state is data stored directly on a blockchain, replicated across every validating node, and verifiable by anyone without trusting a central authority. It is the canonical record of what is true in a blockchain-based system at any given block.

In Ethereum, on-chain state consists of the global state trie — a data structure mapping every account address to its balance, nonce, code hash (for smart contracts), and storage root. Each contract’s storage root points to its own trie of key-value pairs: the persistent variables the contract reads and writes. When a transaction modifies a contract’s variable, every node updates its copy of the state trie and the new Merkle root is recorded in the next block header.

item.title: Proof of Replication
item.description:
Proof of Replication (PoRep) is a cryptographic mechanism where a storage provider proves they are holding a unique, physically distinct copy of data.

How it works:

  1. The provider receives data from a client.
  2. The provider encodes the data through a sequence of graph and hashing operations, creating a “replica” — a sealed, encoded version of the original.
  3. The encoding process is intentionally slow and computationally expensive, making it hard to fake by recomputing on-demand.
  4. The provider publishes a proof that they possess this replica.

Key property: PoRep prevents “Sybil attacks” where a single provider could claim to store multiple copies when they actually store only one. By forcing the encoding step, unique replicas are created and proved.

item.title: Smart Contract
item.description:
A smart contract is a program deployed to a blockchain that executes deterministically when triggered by a transaction. Once deployed, the code cannot be altered unilaterally — it runs exactly as written, enforced by the network’s consensus mechanism rather than by any party to the agreement.

Nick Szabo introduced the term in 1994, defining it as “a computerized transaction protocol that executes the terms of a contract.” Szabo’s insight was that many contractual clauses — collateral, bonding, property delineation — could be embedded in software and hardware, reducing the need for trusted intermediaries. His canonical example was the vending machine: it holds goods, accepts payment, and delivers the product without a shopkeeper.

item.title: Token
item.description:
A token is a unit of value represented by a smart contract on a blockchain. The contract maintains a ledger of balances and exposes functions for transferring ownership. Tokens are not native to the blockchain’s protocol (those are coins, like ETH on Ethereum); they are programs that run on top of it.

Two fundamental categories:

  1. Fungible tokens (ERC-20 on Ethereum): Each unit is interchangeable with every other unit of the same token. One DAI is identical to any other DAI. The contract tracks a single balance per address. Use cases include currencies, governance votes, reward points, and staking deposits.

item.title: Token Inflation
item.description:
Token inflation occurs when the supply of a cryptocurrency grows faster than demand for it, decreasing the value of each token.

Two mechanisms:

  1. Supply growth: New tokens are continuously minted and distributed to users (e.g., farming rewards, staking yield).
  2. Demand insufficiency: The sinks for tokens (ways tokens are destroyed or locked) don’t consume tokens as fast as new ones are minted.

The Axie Infinity case:

Smooth Love Potion (SLP) had an uncapped supply. Supply grew from multiple sources:

item.title: Zero-Knowledge Proof
item.description:
A zero-knowledge proof is a cryptographic protocol in which one party (the prover) convinces another party (the verifier) that a statement is true without conveying any information beyond the truth of that statement.

Properties:

  1. Completeness: If the statement is true, an honest prover can always convince an honest verifier.
  2. Soundness: If the statement is false, no dishonest prover can convince the verifier (except with negligible probability).
  3. Zero knowledge: The verifier learns nothing except that the statement is true.

Common implementations:
